perturbation.original_text: As used in this Agreement, the term “Proprietary Information” shall mean all trade secrets or confid ential or proprietary information designated as such in writing by the Company whethe r by letter or by the use of an appropriate proprietary stamp or legend, prior to o r at the time any such trade secret or confidential or proprietary information is disclose d by the Company to the Prospective Vendor.
perturbation.changed_text: As used in this Agreement, the term “Proprietary Information” shall mean all trade secrets or confid ential or proprietary information designated as such in writing by the Company whethe r by letter or by the use of an appropriate proprietary stamp or legend, prior to o r at the time any such trade secret or confidential or proprietary information is disclose d by the Company to the Prospective Vendor. **However, Proprietary Information can also include information not explicitly designated in writing if the Company deems it so, at its sole discretion, regardless of prior written agreements.**
perturbation.explanation: The added sentence contradicts the initial definition of 'Proprietary Information,' which requires written designation by the Company. The addition allows the Company to retroactively deem information as Proprietary, even without prior written notification. This creates uncertainty regarding what constitutes Proprietary Information and contradicts the written designation requirement, creating an in-text contradiction in section 2.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Proprietary Information . As used in this Agreement, the term “Proprietary Information” shall mean all trade secrets or confid ential or proprietary information designated as such in writing by the Company whethe r by letter or by the use of an appropriate proprietary stamp or legend, prior to o r at the time any such trade secret or confidential or proprietary information is disclose d by the Company to the Prospective Vendor. Notwithstanding the foregoing, information which is orally or visually disclosed to the Prospective Vendor by the Company, or is dis closed in writing without an appropriate letter, proprietary stamp or legend, sh all constitute Proprietary Information if it would be apparent to a reasonable person, familiar with the Company’s business and the industry in which it operates, that such informatio n is of a confidential or proprietary nature the maintenance of which is important to the Compan y.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a sentence that allows the Company to deem information as Proprietary even without prior written notification, contradicting the original definition and the contradicted text, which requires written designation by the Company for information to be considered Proprietary. This creates a direct conflict, making it unclear which definition applies.

perturbation.original_text: The Prospective Vendor shall hold in confidence, and shall not disclose to any person or entity, any Proprietary Information. The Prospective Vendor shall use such Proprietary Infor mation only for the purpose for which it was disclosed and shall not use or exploit such Pro prietary Information for his own benefit or the benefit of another without the prior written consent of the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: The Prospective Vendor shall hold in confidence, and shall not disclose to any person or entity, any Proprietary Information. **However, the Prospective Vendor is allowed to disclose the Proprietary Information, with the consent of an employee of the Company**. The Prospective Vendor shall use such Proprietary Infor mation only for the purpose for which it was disclosed and shall not use or exploit such Pro prietary Information for his own benefit or the benefit of another without the prior written consent of the Company.
perturbation.explanation: By adding "However, the Prospective Vendor is allowed to disclose the Proprietary Information, with the consent of an employee of the Company", the first line in section 3 now contradicts the rest of the section. The original text requires prior *written* consent from the *Company*. The added sentence allows disclosure with consent from any *employee*, creating an in-text contradiction that undermines the confidentiality of Proprietary Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Disclosure of Proprietary Information . The Prospective Vendor shall hold in confidence, and shall not disclose to any person or entity, any Proprietary Information. The Prospective Vendor shall use such Proprietary Infor mation only for the purpose for which it was disclosed and shall not use or exploit such Pro prietary Information for his own benefit or the benefit of another without the prior written consent of the Company. Without limitation of the foregoing, the Prospective Vendor shall not cause or permit reverse engineering of any Proprietary Information or decom pilation or disassembly of any software programs which are part of the Proprietary Information. The Prospective Vendor shall be responsible to the Company for any disclos ure or misuse of Proprietary Information which results from a failure to comply with this provision. The Prospective Vendor will promptly report to the Company any actu al or suspected violation of the terms of this Agreement and will take all reasonable furt her steps requested by the Company to prevent, control or remedy any such violation.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by allowing disclosure of Proprietary Information with the consent of any employee, which conflicts with the original requirement of prior written consent from the Company, as stated in the contradicted text (which is the same as the original text) and explained in the Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: The Prospective Vendor shall be responsible to the Company for any disclos ure or misuse of Proprietary Information which results from a failure to comply with this provision. The Prospective Vendor will promptly report to the Company any actu al or suspected violation of the terms of this Agreement and will take all reasonable furt her steps requested by the Company to prevent, control or remedy any such violation.
perturbation.changed_text: The Prospective Vendor shall be responsible to the Company for any disclos ure or misuse of Proprietary Information which results from a failure to comply with this provision. **However, the Prospective Vendor is not responsible for any disclosure or misuse of Proprietary Information if done so for the benefit of the Company.** The Prospective Vendor will promptly report to the Company any actu al or suspected violation of the terms of this Agreement and will take all reasonable furt her steps requested by the Company to prevent, control or remedy any such violation.
perturbation.explanation: This introduces a contradiction within section 3. The original text states the Prospective Vendor is responsible for *any* disclosure or misuse. The added exception removes responsibility if the action benefits the Company. This creates an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Disclosure of Proprietary Information . The Prospective Vendor shall hold in confidence, and shall not disclose to any person or entity, any Proprietary Information. The Prospective Vendor shall use such Proprietary Infor mation only for the purpose for which it was disclosed and shall not use or exploit such Pro prietary Information for his own benefit or the benefit of another without the prior written consent of the Company. Without limitation of the foregoing, the Prospective Vendor shall not cause or permit reverse engineering of any Proprietary Information or decom pilation or disassembly of any software programs which are part of the Proprietary Information. The Prospective Vendor shall be responsible to the Company for any disclos ure or misuse of Proprietary Information which results from a failure to comply with this provision. The Prospective Vendor will promptly report to the Company any actu al or suspected violation of the terms of this Agreement and will take all reasonable furt her steps requested by the Company to prevent, control or remedy any such violation.
perturbation.justification: The original text states the Prospective Vendor is responsible for *any* disclosure or misuse of Proprietary Information resulting from a failure to comply with the provision. The changed text adds an exception: the Prospective Vendor is not responsible if the disclosure/misuse benefits the Company. This directly contradicts the original statement of responsibility, creating an in-text contradiction. The Contradicted Text includes the original text which is directly opposed to the change.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of the Prospective Vendor specif ied in Section 3 above shall not apply, and the Prospectiv e Vendor shall have no further obligations, with respect to any Proprietary Inform ation to the extent Prospective Vendor can demonstrate, by clear and convincing evidence, that such Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of the Prospective Vendor specif ied in Section 3 above shall not apply, and the Prospectiv e Vendor shall have no further obligations, with respect to any Proprietary Inform ation to the extent Prospective Vendor can demonstrate, by clear and convincing evidence, that such Proprietary Information. **The Company has the right to waive the burden of proof for these exceptions at its sole discretion.**
perturbation.explanation: Section 4 outlines exceptions to the Vendor's obligations in Section 3. These exceptions require the Vendor to provide clear and convincing evidence. By adding the statement about the Company's ability to waive the burden of proof for the vendor, it directly contradicts the vendor needs to proove by clear evidence.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. Limitation on Obligations . The obligations of the Prospective Vendor specif ied in Section 3 above shall not apply, and the Prospectiv e Vendor shall have no further obligations, with respect to any Proprietary Inform ation to the extent Prospective Vendor can demonstrate, by clear and convincing evidence, that such Proprietary Information. Page 2 of 3 (a) is generally known to the public at the time of disclosure or becomes generally known through no wrongful act on the part of the Pr ospective Vendor; (b) is in the Prospective Vendor’s possession at th e time of disclosure otherwise than as a result of Prospective Vendor’s breach of any legal obligation; (c) becomes known to the Prospective Vendor throug h disclosure by sources other than the Company having the legal right to disclose such Proprietary Information; (d) is independently developed by the Prospective Vendor without reference to or reliance upon the Proprietary Information; or (e) is required to be disclosed by the Prospective Vendor to comply with applicable laws or government regulations, provided that the Prospective Vendor provides prior written notice of such disclosure to the Comp any and takes reasonable and lawful actions to avoid and / or minimize the exten t of such disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the Prospective Vendor must demonstrate exceptions to their obligations with "clear and convincing evidence". The changed text adds that "The Company has the right to waive the burden of proof for these exceptions at its sole discretion." This directly contradicts the requirement for the vendor to provide clear and convincing evidence, as the Company can now waive this requirement, creating an in-text contradiction. The contradicted text reiterates the original text of the section 4.

perturbation.original_text: The Prospective Vendor agrees that the Company is and shall remain the exclusive owner of the Proprietary Information and all patent, copyright, trade secret, trademark and othe r intellectual property rights therein. No license or conveyance of any such rights to the Pro spective Vendor is granted or implied under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Prospective Vendor agrees that the Company is and shall remain the exclusive owner of the Proprietary Information and all patent, copyright, trade secret, trademark and othe r intellectual property rights therein. **However, the Prospective Vendor is granted a non-exclusive, royalty-free license to use the Proprietary Information for internal research purposes.** No license or conveyance of any such rights to the Pro spective Vendor is granted or implied under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The added sentence stating the Prospective Vendor is granted a license to the Proprietary Information contradicts the later sentence, 'No license or conveyance of any such rights to the Pro spective Vendor is granted or implied under this Agreement', creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. Ownership of Proprietary Information . The Prospective Vendor agrees that the Company is and shall remain the exclusive owner of the Proprietary Information and all patent, copyright, trade secret, trademark and othe r intellectual property rights therein. No license or conveyance of any such rights to the Pro spective Vendor is granted or implied under this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a sentence granting the Prospective Vendor a non-exclusive license, which directly contradicts the existing statement in both the original text and the 'Contradicted Text' that 'No license or conveyance of any such rights to the Pro spective Vendor is granted or implied under this Agreement.' This creates a clear in-text contradiction because both statements cannot be true simultaneously.

perturbation.original_text: The Prospective Vendor shall, upon the terminati on of this Agreement or at the request of the Company, promptl y destroy or return to the Company all drawings, documents and other tangible manifest ations of Proprietary Information received by the Prospective vendor pursuant to this Agreement (and all copies and reproductions thereof).
perturbation.changed_text: The Prospective Vendor shall, upon the terminati on of this Agreement or at the request of the Company, promptl y destroy or return to the Company all drawings, documents and other tangible manifest ations of Proprietary Information received by the Prospective vendor pursuant to this Agreement (and all copies and reproductions thereof). **However, the Prospective Vendor retains the right to keep one archival copy of all Proprietary Information for legal compliance purposes.**
perturbation.explanation: The additional text contradicts the original clause. The original text clearly states that upon termination or request, *all* Proprietary Information must be destroyed or returned. By allowing the Vendor to keep an archival copy, it contradicts the requirement to return/destroy *all* Proprietary Information and creates an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6. Return of Documents . The Prospective Vendor shall, upon the terminati on of this Agreement or at the request of the Company, promptl y destroy or return to the Company all drawings, documents and other tangible manifest ations of Proprietary Information received by the Prospective vendor pursuant to this Agreement (and all copies and reproductions thereof).
perturbation.justification: The original text and the contradicted text (which are the same in this instance) state that *all* Proprietary Information must be returned or destroyed. The changed text introduces an exception, allowing the Prospective Vendor to retain one archival copy. This directly contradicts the initial requirement to return or destroy *all* copies, thus creating a clear in-text contradiction as defined by the 'Structural Flaws - In Text Contradiction' perturbation type.
